splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate an employer's withdrawal liability from a multiemployer pension plan to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether the actuary may update them afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be established by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select or update actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee is a fugitive, so the release term does not run while the person absconds.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of Section 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits raising any claim previously presented — in addition to the separate requirements of Section 2255(h) when bringing successive collateral attacks. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from re-raising claims; if not, they need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the gatekeeping bar of § 2244(b)(1), which prohibits relitigating previously raised claims, applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only meet § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state voting procedures. Some circuits have found standing based on competitive injury theories, while others have required more concrete harm. The resolution affects the ability to challenge mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate suffers a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when a state law allegedly dilutes lawful votes by permitting the counting of late-arriving ballots.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized competitive concerns about ballot-counting procedures are insufficient to establish the concrete, particularized injury required by Article III.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to an agency's or the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of ambiguous provisions in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while others (including the Third Circuit panel in Chandler) have held that deference trumps lenity. The split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over Auer/Kisor or Chevron deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes priority over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines enhancements or similar criminal provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means 'and' or 'or.' The Ninth and Eleventh Circuits hold defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria, while the Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth Circuits hold that possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they satisfy all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' conjunctively.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' disjunctively.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2 — which adds 'aiding and abetting, conspiring, and attempting' to the definition of 'controlled substance offense' — is a valid, authoritative interpretation of the guideline or impermissibly expands it beyond its text. This affects career-offender designations and significantly impacts sentencing ranges.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is authoritative — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a valid interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) under Stinson deference and that conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Application Note 1 exceeds guideline text — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning, especially after Kisor v. Wilkie.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive statutory sentencing changes — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory stacked § 924(c) sentences — as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits bar any consideration of such changes, while others permit it as one factor among several. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to resolve this but has itself generated disagreement about the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot constitute or contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), because allowing it would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered as a factor
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute covers substances not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the state offense to involve a substance also controlled under federal law, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies. The Eighth Circuit holds no federal-law limitation applies, while other circuits have required correspondence to federal schedules.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation — any state drug offense qualifies
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any 'offense under state law,' and there is no textual basis to require that the substance also be controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Must correspond to federal schedules
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance at issue is also controlled under federal law, applying a categorical approach that requires a match to federal schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's assets belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor when a Chapter 13 case is converted to Chapter 7 in good faith.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A). The Ninth Circuit held the increased equity belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that protect debtors' post-petition appreciation upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity are 'property of the estate' under § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a), and thus belong to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects debtors who convert in good faith by limiting the estate to property as valued on the original petition date, so post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the longer civil notice-of-appeal deadline) or governed by the criminal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but noted a lopsided circuit split exists on the question.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil notice-of-appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal proceeding and is governed by the shorter 14-day criminal notice-of-appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration of custody exclusively through habeas corpus, or whether they may also use civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens or the APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists over whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions that affect the fact or duration of confinement (such as First Step Act time credit calculations), or whether prisoners may alternatively bring claims under the APA or other civil rights statutes. This affects prisoners' access to federal courts for a wide range of BOP-related grievances.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-of-custody challenges
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's claim challenges the fact or duration of confinement, habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy and civil rights or APA actions are not available alternatives.

positions.label: Civil rights or APA actions may also be available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring conditions-of-confinement or BOP decision challenges through civil rights actions or the APA, not solely through habeas, particularly when the claims do not directly challenge the fact of confinement.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for the conduct underlying a supervised release violation when revoking supervised release and imposing a new sentence.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider punishment for the violative conduct as a purpose of the sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit under United States v. Lewis allows courts to punish for the underlying violation, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences should focus on the breach of trust and not punish conduct already subject to separate prosecution.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that upon revoking supervised release, district courts may expressly consider punishment of the defendant for the conduct underlying the violation when imposing a new sentence.

positions.label: Revocation sentences should not punish the new conduct
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of a revocation sentence is to sanction the breach of the court's trust inherent in the violation of supervised release conditions, not to punish the defendant for the underlying criminal conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the context of hospitality and business establishments, including burden-shifting frameworks and the availability of certain damages?
splits.description: The Second Circuit's approach to § 1981 discrimination claims in the hospitality context was found sufficiently unsettled to warrant Supreme Court review, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards. The case will clarify standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages across service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit's approach
positions.summary: Applied its own framework for evaluating § 1981 claims in the hospitality context, the specifics of which SCOTUS found warranted review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for proving and evaluating discrimination claims under § 1981 in commercial settings, creating the split that prompted certiorari.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause — barring states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker' — preempts common-law negligent hiring or selection claims against freight brokers when the broker's chosen carrier causes injury. This affects tort liability across the freight industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' broker services and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA's preemption provision.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that general negligence claims against freight brokers are not 'related to' broker services within the meaning of the FAAAA and therefore are not preempted.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may encompass claims that overlap with or are traditionally raised in habeas petitions under § 2255, such as arguments that a conviction or sentence was legally erroneous. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider legal errors as a factor
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider potential legal errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the totality of circumstances when evaluating extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not use compassionate release to remedy legal errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a proper vehicle for claims challenging the legality of a conviction or sentence, which must be brought through § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
